children.name: Grus
children.description: The true cranes — tall, long-legged birds of open wetlands and grasslands whose bugling calls and spectacular migration concentrations make them among the most iconic of all Palearctic birds. The common crane (trana) is Sweden's defining migratory spectacle: each April, over 100,000 cranes converge on Lake Hornborga in Västergötland to rest and refuel before continuing north to their breeding grounds, making it one of the greatest wildlife events in Europe. Cranes are among the longest-lived birds, forming pair bonds that last decades and engaging in elaborate dancing displays — leaping, wing-spreading, and bowing — that strengthen their partnership. In Sweden, the crane's resonant, bugling 'trrop trrop' call is so deeply embedded in the national consciousness that it has become synonymous with the arrival of spring.

children.name: Grus grus
children.description: The trana — Sweden's most beloved migratory bird. Breeds in bogs and swamp forests across Sweden from Skåne to Lapland, with a population of around 80,000 pairs. The Hornborga gathering each April draws over 20,000 human visitors and is one of Scandinavia's premier wildlife events. Cranes mate for life; pairs perform elaborate dancing ceremonies year-round to reinforce the bond. Their loud, trumpeting calls, produced by a convoluted trachea coiled inside the breastbone, carry for kilometres across open farmland.

children.name: Grus americana
children.description: The tallest bird in North America at 1.5 m, brilliant white with a red crown and black wingtips. Once on the brink of extinction — just 15 individuals remained in 1941 — a decades-long recovery programme has brought the wild population to over 800. Breeds only in Wood Buffalo National Park, Canada, wintering in Aransas, Texas; its epic 4,000 km migration is one of conservation's greatest success stories.

children.name: Grus japonensis
children.description: The largest crane in the world by weight and one of the rarest, Endangered with about 3,000 remaining. A powerful symbol of longevity, good fortune, and fidelity in Japanese, Chinese, and Korean culture. The red crown patch is bare skin rather than feathers. Breeds in Hokkaido (Japan) and northeastern China; two distinct populations, one migratory and one resident.

children.name: Grus monacha
children.description: A grey crane with a white head and black forehead, breeding in remote larch taiga bogs of Siberia. Winters in spectacular concentrations at Arasaki in Kyushu, Japan, and at several South Korean sites. Vulnerable; its remote breeding sites are poorly monitored, and wintering concentrations make the species vulnerable to disease outbreaks.

children.name: Grus nigricollis
children.description: The only crane endemic to the Tibetan Plateau and the only crane that breeds at high altitude, nesting in wetlands at 3,000–5,000 m elevation. Venerated by Tibetan Buddhists who consider it sacred. Vulnerable; a population of around 10,000–15,000 is threatened by habitat degradation, infrastructure development, and climate change on the plateau.

children.name: Anthropoides
children.description: Demoiselle cranes and blue cranes — elegant, relatively small cranes of dry steppes and semi-arid grasslands far removed from the wetland habitats favoured by most of their relatives. The demoiselle crane is the world's smallest crane, breeding across the central Asian steppes from the Black Sea to Mongolia and wintering in India. Its name comes from French — given by Marie Antoinette who was charmed by its dainty, graceful appearance — and it is indeed a bird of extraordinary elegance: grey-bodied with a long white ear-tuft, a black neck and breast, and long decorative tertial feathers that droop over the tail. Demoiselle cranes are rare but annual vagrants to Scandinavia, typically appearing in spring among flocks of common cranes at staging sites such as Lake Hornborga.

children.name: Anthropoides virgo
children.description: An elegant vagrant from the central Asian steppes, seen annually in small numbers among common crane flocks at Hornborga and other Swedish staging sites. Distinctly smaller and more delicate than the common crane, with a black breast, white ear-tufts, and long ornamental tertials. Makes one of the most dramatic migrations of any bird, crossing the Himalayas at extreme altitude to reach Indian wintering grounds. Its name — French for 'young lady' — reflects the grace that enchanted 18th-century European courts.

children.name: Anthropoides paradiseus
children.description: South Africa's national bird — a pale blue-grey crane with long, trailing secondary feathers that sweep the ground like a bridal train. Almost entirely restricted to South Africa, where it inhabits dry grassland and farmland rather than wetlands. Vulnerable; its population of around 26,000 has declined sharply due to poisoning by crop-protecting chemicals, powerline collisions, and habitat loss.

children.name: Antigone
children.description: A genus of large cranes of Asia and Australasia separated from Grus on molecular and morphological grounds. Includes the world's tallest flying bird (the Sarus Crane) and the iconic Australian Brolga. These cranes tend to inhabit tropical and subtropical wetlands, rice paddies, and grasslands and perform spectacular communal dancing displays.

children.name: Antigone antigone
children.description: The world's tallest flying bird, standing up to 1.8 m tall with a 2.4 m wingspan. Sacred in India, where pairs are believed to mate for life and their loss is an omen of ill fortune; the species features prominently in Hindu texts. Vulnerable; populations have declined across South and Southeast Asia due to wetland drainage, pesticide use, and hunting.

children.name: Antigone canadensis
children.description: The world's most numerous crane, with a population exceeding 800,000. Forms the most spectacular of all crane migrations: each spring, up to 500,000 birds concentrate on a 150 km stretch of the Platte River in Nebraska for three to four weeks. Highly adaptable; inhabits tundra, grasslands, wetlands, and agricultural fields from Alaska to Cuba.

children.name: Antigone rubicunda
children.description: Australia's tallest native bird and a cultural icon, featuring in Aboriginal stories and art for millennia. Performs elaborate communal dancing in groups — leaping, flapping wings, and calling — a behaviour the name 'brolga' reflects from the Gamilaraay language word. Found in tropical and subtropical wetlands across northern and eastern Australia, with a small population in southern New Guinea.

children.name: Antigone vipio
children.description: A large crane with a distinctive grey body, white nape and neck stripes, and a bare red facial patch. Breeds in the grasslands and wetlands of Mongolia, northeastern China, and adjacent Russia. Vulnerable; the entire population of around 5,500 winters at just a handful of sites in Japan and the Korean peninsula, making it acutely vulnerable to disturbance.

children.name: Leucogeranus
children.description: Monotypic genus for the Siberian Crane, a distinctive all-white crane of Arctic Siberia. Separated from Grus by its brick-red bare facial skin and molecular differences. The Western and Central Asian populations are functionally extinct as migratory populations; only the Eastern population — wintering at Poyang Lake, China — survives in any numbers.

children.name: Leucogeranus leucogeranus
children.description: Critically Endangered; the wild population numbers approximately 4,000, almost entirely at the Poyang Lake wintering site in China. Historically three separate populations existed — Western (wintering in Iran and India), Central (Afghanistan), and Eastern (China) — but only the Eastern survives. Undertakes one of the longest migrations of any crane, traversing Central Asia between Arctic Siberian breeding grounds and Yangtze floodplains.

children.name: Balearica
children.description: The crowned cranes of Africa — the most ancient living crane lineage and the only cranes that can perch in trees, roosting in acacia trees and canopy. Distinguished by their spectacular golden straw crown, red and white cheek patches, and red throat pouch. Crowned cranes are the only cranes that breed in sub-Saharan Africa and they hold cultural significance across much of the continent.

children.name: Balearica pavonina
children.description: Found across the Sahel and savanna belt from Senegal to Ethiopia and Sudan, the national bird of Nigeria and the national bird of Sudan. The black body, white wing panels, and golden crown make it unmistakeable. Vulnerable; populations have declined due to wetland drainage, agricultural conversion, and capture for the live bird trade.

children.name: Balearica regulorum
children.description: National bird of Uganda, depicted on its flag and coat of arms. Inhabits wetlands, grasslands, and agricultural land in East and southern Africa from Kenya to South Africa. Endangered; the population has fallen by around 50% in three generations due to wetland destruction, poisoning, and illegal capture for sale as pets or for traditional ceremonies.

children.name: Bugeranus
children.description: Monotypic genus of the Wattled Crane, Africa's largest crane and one of its most threatened birds. Named for the distinctive fleshy red wattles hanging from the chin and throat. Found in large Afromontane wetlands and floodplains in sub-Saharan Africa, with strongholds in Zambia, Ethiopia, and South Africa.

children.name: Bugeranus carunculatus
children.description: Africa's largest crane, standing 1.2 m tall, with grey plumage, a white neck, and distinctive red facial wattles that grow larger with age. Vulnerable; fewer than 8,000 remain, fragmented across sub-Saharan wetlands. Breeds in large marshes and floodplains; highly sensitive to wetland disturbance and has one of the lowest reproductive rates of all cranes.
